title: The night train to Prague
summary: Two strangers share a compartment on a delayed night train and disagree about whether the delay is good or bad.

body.speaker: Marek
body.text: Excuse me, is this seat free? The train is very full tonight.

body.speaker: Ingrid
body.text: Yes, please sit. But I should tell you: we are not moving. The train stopped twenty minutes ago. There is snow on the line near the border.

body.speaker: Marek
body.text: Oh no. I have a meeting in Prague at nine o'clock in the morning. If we do not move soon, I will miss it.

body.speaker: Ingrid
body.text: I am sorry. For me it is different. I am in no hurry. I think a stopped train at night is a small gift.

body.speaker: Marek
body.text: A gift? I am losing money every minute we sit here.

body.speaker: Ingrid
body.text: Look out of the window. The snow is falling on a dark field. There are no cars, no phones ringing, no emails. The world is waiting too. When the train moves, all of that comes back.

body.speaker: Marek
body.text: You are calm because you have nowhere to be.

body.speaker: Ingrid
body.text: That is true. But you are not calm because you think the meeting is your whole life. Tomorrow you will be in the meeting, and you will not remember the snow.

body.speaker: Marek
body.text: [He is quiet for a moment.] Maybe. My grandfather drove a train on this line. He said the best moments were the stops, not the speed.

body.speaker: Ingrid
body.text: Your grandfather and I would agree. Here, I have tea in this flask. The meeting will still be there at nine. The snow will not.

body.speaker: Marek
body.text: All right. One cup. And then, if the train still does not move, you can tell me about the field.

annotations.meaningVi: chúng ta không di chuyển (tàu đứng yên)

annotations.phrase: the best moments were the stops
annotations.meaningVi: những khoảnh khắc đẹp nhất là lúc dừng lại

grammarNotes.title: First conditional: 'if + present, will + verb'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng câu điều kiện loại 1 để nói về kết quả có thể xảy ra trong tương lai. Trong bài: 'If we do not move soon, I will miss it.' Cấu trúc: 'If + thì hiện tại đơn, chủ ngữ + will + động từ nguyên thể.' Mệnh đề 'if' dùng hiện tại đơn (không dùng 'will'); mệnh đề chính dùng 'will'. Có thể đảo: 'I will miss it if we do not move.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses the first conditional for a likely future result. From the dialogue: 'If we do not move soon, I will miss it.' Structure: 'If + present simple, subject + will + base verb.' The 'if' clause takes the present simple (not 'will'); the main clause takes 'will'. Order can switch: 'I will miss it if we do not move.'

translationVi:
Marek: Xin lỗi, ghế này còn trống không? Tàu tối nay rất đông.

Ingrid: Vâng, mời anh ngồi. Nhưng tôi nên nói cho anh biết: chúng ta không di chuyển. Tàu dừng hai mươi phút trước rồi. Có tuyết trên đường ray gần biên giới.

Marek: Ôi không. Tôi có một cuộc họp ở Prague lúc chín giờ sáng. Nếu chúng ta không đi sớm, tôi sẽ lỡ nó.

Ingrid: Tôi rất tiếc. Với tôi thì khác. Tôi không vội. Tôi nghĩ một con tàu dừng lại trong đêm là một món quà nhỏ.

Marek: Một món quà? Cứ mỗi phút ngồi đây tôi lại mất tiền.

Ingrid: Hãy nhìn ra cửa sổ. Tuyết đang rơi trên một cánh đồng tối. Không có xe, không có điện thoại reo, không có email. Cả thế giới cũng đang chờ. Khi tàu chạy, tất cả những thứ đó sẽ quay lại.

Marek: Cô thư thái vì cô chẳng phải đến đâu cả.

Ingrid: Đúng vậy. Nhưng anh không thư thái vì anh nghĩ cuộc họp là cả cuộc đời anh. Ngày mai anh sẽ ở trong cuộc họp, và anh sẽ không nhớ tuyết.

Marek: [Anh im lặng một lát.] Có thể. Ông tôi từng lái tàu trên tuyến này. Ông nói khoảnh khắc đẹp nhất là lúc dừng, không phải lúc chạy nhanh.

Ingrid: Ông anh và tôi chắc sẽ đồng ý với nhau. Đây, tôi có trà trong bình này. Cuộc họp vẫn sẽ ở đó lúc chín giờ. Tuyết thì không.

Marek: Thôi được. Một cốc thôi. Và rồi, nếu tàu vẫn không chạy, cô có thể kể cho tôi nghe về cánh đồng.

options: There is snow on the line near the border.
It is the end of the line.
questions.explanation: 'There is snow on the line near the border.'

options: He has a meeting in Prague at nine in the morning.
He left his bag at home.
questions.explanation: 'I have a meeting in Prague at nine o'clock.'

questions.prompt: How does Ingrid feel about the delay?
options: She thinks a stopped train at night is a small gift.
She wants to get off and walk.
questions.explanation: 'I think a stopped train at night is a small gift.'

questions.prompt: What does Ingrid tell Marek to do?
options: Look out of the window at the snow.
questions.explanation: 'Look out of the window. The snow is falling.'

questions.prompt: What does Marek say is happening to him each minute?
questions.explanation: 'I am losing money every minute we sit here.'

questions.prompt: What does Ingrid say about Marek's calm?
options: He is not calm because he thinks the meeting is his whole life.
He should never have a meeting.
questions.explanation: 'You think the meeting is your whole life.'

options: He drove a train on this line.
questions.explanation: 'My grandfather drove a train on this line.'

questions.prompt: What did the grandfather say about the train?
options: The best moments were the stops, not the speed.
questions.explanation: 'The best moments were the stops, not the speed.'

questions.prompt: What does Marek finally agree to?
options: To have one cup of tea.

questions.prompt: What does Ingrid mean by 'The meeting will still be there at nine. The snow will not'?
options: The rare, passing moment matters more right now than the fixed plan.
The snow will melt by nine.
She wants to leave before nine.
questions.explanation: She contrasts the permanent task with the fleeting view.

options: Two strangers hold opposite views of a delay, and one slowly opens the other to seeing the pause as something worth having.
Meetings are a waste of time.
questions.explanation: The disagreement softens into shared tea and a borrowed view.

blanks.explanation: Drove a train on this line.
I have tea in this flask.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Ingrid says Marek is upset because he treats one meeting as his 'whole life'. Think of a recent delay that annoyed you. Was the thing you were rushing to really as large as it felt — and did you miss anything by rushing?
